title: CompTIA Security+ (SY0-701) Practice Exam
description: A 90-question practice exam modeled after the CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 objectives, covering General Security Concepts, Threats/Vulnerabilities/Mitigations, Security Architecture, Security Operations, and Security Program Management & Oversight.

questions.question: Q: Which of the CIA triad principles is primarily concerned with ensuring that data has not been altered in an unauthorized manner?
questions.explanation: Integrity ensures that data remains accurate and unaltered by unauthorized parties. Hashing and digital signatures are common controls used to verify integrity.
questions.hint: Think about which control detects tampering with a file.

questions.question: Q: A security control that is implemented after an incident has occurred to reduce its impact is BEST described as which control type?
questions.explanation: Corrective controls act after an event to restore systems and limit damage, such as restoring from backup or applying a patch. Preventive controls stop events, detective controls identify them, and deterrent controls discourage them.
questions.hint: It happens after the fact to fix things.

questions.question: Q: Which access control model assigns permissions based on the sensitivity labels of objects and the clearance of subjects?
questions.explanation: MAC uses labels (e.g., Top Secret, Secret) and clearances enforced by the system, not the resource owner. It is common in military and government environments.
questions.hint: This model is common in military and classified environments.

questions.question: Q: An organization wants to ensure a sender cannot later deny having sent a message. Which security concept does this provide?
questions.explanation: Non-repudiation prevents a party from denying an action. Digital signatures provide non-repudiation because only the sender holds the private key used to sign.
questions.hint: A digital signature provides this.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of a honeypot?
options: A: To encrypt data at rest
B: To lure and observe attackers away from production systems
questions.answer: B: To lure and observe attackers away from production systems
questions.explanation: A honeypot is a decoy system designed to attract attackers so their techniques can be studied and to divert them from real assets. A honeynet is a network of honeypots.
questions.hint: It is a decoy meant to attract attackers.

questions.question: Q: A company uses a device to keep time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) generated for logins. This is an example of which authentication factor?
questions.explanation: A hardware or software token that generates one-time codes is a possession factor — 'something you have.' Combining it with a password creates multifactor authentication.
questions.hint: You physically possess the token.

questions.question: Q: Which encryption approach uses the SAME key to encrypt and decrypt data?
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption (e.g., AES) uses a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption, making it fast but requiring secure key distribution.
questions.hint: One shared secret key does both operations.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is an example of a technical (logical) security control?
options: A: A security awareness training program
B: A firewall access control list
questions.answer: B: A firewall access control list
questions.explanation: Technical controls are implemented through technology, such as firewalls, encryption, and access control lists. Training and policies are managerial/administrative, and fences are physical.
questions.hint: It is enforced through hardware or software.

questions.question: Q: An attacker sends emails that appear to come from a company's CEO to trick finance staff into wiring funds. What type of attack is this?
questions.explanation: Whaling is a phishing attack that targets high-profile individuals or impersonates executives. Vishing uses voice, smishing uses SMS, and shoulder surfing is observing directly.
questions.hint: It targets the 'big fish' executives.

questions.question: Q: Which document is a formal agreement between two parties that defines the responsibilities and expectations but is NOT legally binding?
questions.explanation: A Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) documents intentions and expectations between parties and is generally not legally binding. An SLA defines service levels, a BPA governs a business partnership, and an NDA protects confidentiality.
questions.hint: It expresses mutual intentions but lacks legal enforceability.

questions.question: Q: A security analyst notices outbound traffic to a known malicious IP occurring every 60 seconds from an internal host. This behavior MOST likely indicates which of the following?
questions.explanation: Regular, periodic outbound connections to a malicious host are characteristic of C2 beaconing, where malware checks in with its controller for instructions.
questions.hint: Malware 'phones home' on a regular interval.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the principle of least privilege?
options: A: Users should have administrative rights for convenience
B: Users should be granted only the access necessary to perform their job
C: All users should share a single account
D: Access should be based solely on seniority
questions.answer: B: Users should be granted only the access necessary to perform their job
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits users, processes, and systems to the minimum access required, reducing the attack surface and potential damage from compromise.
questions.hint: Give only what is needed, nothing more.

questions.question: Q: A penetration tester is given no prior knowledge of the target environment. Which type of test is being performed?
questions.explanation: In a black box test, the tester has no internal knowledge, simulating an external attacker. White box provides full information, and gray box provides partial information.
questions.hint: The tester works in the dark, like an outsider.

questions.question: Q: Which cryptographic concept ensures that even if a session key is compromised, past session keys remain secure?
questions.explanation: Perfect forward secrecy (PFS) generates unique ephemeral keys per session, so compromising one key does not expose previous or future sessions.
questions.hint: Each session uses a unique ephemeral key.

questions.question: Q: An organization must comply with a regulation requiring cardholder data protection. Which framework applies?
questions.explanation: The Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) governs the protection of cardholder data. HIPAA covers health data, SOX covers financial reporting, and FERPA covers education records.
questions.hint: It is specific to credit card data.

questions.question: Q: Which type of malware disguises itself as legitimate software to trick users into installing it?
questions.explanation: A Trojan appears to be legitimate but carries a malicious payload. Unlike a worm, it does not self-replicate; it relies on the user to run it.
questions.hint: Named after a famous Greek deception.

questions.question: Q: A company implements salting for stored passwords. What is the PRIMARY purpose of a salt?
options: A: To encrypt the password with AES
B: To add unique random data so identical passwords hash differently
D: To speed up hash computation
questions.answer: B: To add unique random data so identical passwords hash differently
questions.explanation: A salt is random data added before hashing so that identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow table attacks.
questions.hint: It defeats rainbow tables.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a characteristic of a zero-day vulnerability?
options: A: It has a vendor patch available
B: It is unknown to the vendor and has no available patch
C: It only affects legacy systems
D: It is always a physical security flaw
questions.answer: B: It is unknown to the vendor and has no available patch
questions.explanation: A zero-day vulnerability is unknown to the vendor, meaning no patch exists and defenders have 'zero days' to prepare before it can be exploited.
questions.hint: The vendor has had zero days to fix it.

questions.question: Q: Which technology allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical host by abstracting hardware resources?
questions.explanation: A hypervisor abstracts physical hardware to run multiple virtual machines, each with its own OS. Containers share the host OS kernel instead.
questions.hint: A hypervisor makes this possible.

questions.question: Q: A security team wants to detect malicious activity by comparing behavior against a known-good baseline. Which detection method is this?
questions.explanation: Anomaly-based detection establishes a baseline of normal behavior and flags deviations. Signature-based detection matches known patterns of malicious activity.
questions.hint: It flags deviations from 'normal.'

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST example of multifactor authentication?
options: A: A password and a PIN
B: A fingerprint and a retina scan
C: A password and a smart card
questions.answer: C: A password and a smart card
questions.explanation: MFA requires factors from different categories. A password (something you know) plus a smart card (something you have) qualifies. Two knowledge factors or two biometrics do not.
questions.hint: The factors must be from different categories.

questions.question: Q: Which attack manipulates a database query by inserting malicious input into an application's data fields?
questions.explanation: SQL injection inserts crafted SQL into input fields to manipulate database queries, potentially exposing or altering data. Input validation and parameterized queries mitigate it.
questions.hint: The attack targets the backend database.

questions.question: Q: A security architect wants to segment the network so that a compromised IoT device cannot reach critical servers. Which control BEST accomplishes this?
options: B: Network segmentation with VLANs and ACLs
questions.answer: B: Network segmentation with VLANs and ACLs
questions.explanation: Segmentation isolates devices into separate zones so lateral movement is restricted. VLANs plus ACLs enforce boundaries between IoT and critical systems.
questions.hint: You want to isolate device groups from each other.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes a supply chain attack?
options: A: Flooding a server with traffic
B: Compromising a trusted third-party vendor to reach the target
questions.answer: B: Compromising a trusted third-party vendor to reach the target
questions.explanation: A supply chain attack compromises a trusted supplier, software update, or hardware component to infiltrate downstream victims — as seen in software-update poisoning incidents.
questions.hint: The attacker enters through a trusted vendor.

questions.question: Q: What is the PRIMARY purpose of a Data Loss Prevention (DLP) solution?
options: A: To back up data automatically
B: To detect and prevent unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data
questions.answer: B: To detect and prevent unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data
questions.explanation: DLP monitors data in use, in motion, and at rest to prevent sensitive information from leaving the organization without authorization.
questions.hint: It stops sensitive data from leaving the org.

questions.question: Q: An administrator disables unused ports and services on a server before deployment. This process is BEST described as which of the following?
questions.explanation: System hardening reduces the attack surface by disabling unnecessary services, closing ports, removing default accounts, and applying secure configurations.
questions.hint: Reducing the attack surface of a system.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between RTO and RPO?
options: A: RTO is the acceptable data loss; RPO is the recovery time
B: RTO is the target recovery time; RPO is the acceptable amount of data loss
D: RTO measures uptime; RPO measures downtime
questions.answer: B: RTO is the target recovery time; RPO is the acceptable amount of data loss
questions.explanation: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) is how quickly systems must be restored. Recovery Point Objective (RPO) is how much data loss (measured in time) is tolerable.
questions.hint: One measures time to recover; the other measures data loss.

questions.question: Q: A user reports their screen displays a message demanding payment to unlock their encrypted files. Which malware is responsible?
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts a victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key. Reliable, tested offline backups are the strongest defense.
questions.hint: It holds your files hostage for money.

questions.question: Q: Which protocol provides secure, encrypted remote command-line access to a device?
questions.explanation: Secure Shell (SSH) encrypts remote administrative sessions on port 22. Telnet and FTP transmit data in cleartext and should be avoided.
questions.hint: It replaces Telnet with encryption.

questions.question: Q: What is the primary function of a Certificate Authority (CA) in a PKI?
options: A: To generate symmetric keys for all users
B: To issue and digitally sign certificates that bind identities to public keys
questions.answer: B: To issue and digitally sign certificates that bind identities to public keys
questions.explanation: A CA validates identities and issues digitally signed certificates that associate a public key with an entity, forming the trust foundation of PKI.
questions.hint: It vouches for the ownership of public keys.

questions.question: Q: An attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties to intercept and alter traffic. What is this attack called?
questions.explanation: An on-path attack (formerly man-in-the-middle) intercepts communications between two parties, allowing eavesdropping or manipulation. Encryption and certificate validation mitigate it.
questions.hint: The attacker sits in the middle of the conversation.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is an example of a compensating control?
options: A: The primary control that fully meets a requirement
B: An alternative control used when the primary control cannot be implemented
C: A control that has been decommissioned
D: A control applied only to physical assets
questions.answer: B: An alternative control used when the primary control cannot be implemented
questions.explanation: A compensating control provides an alternative means of meeting a security requirement when the standard control is not feasible, offering comparable protection.
questions.hint: It substitutes when the ideal control isn't possible.

questions.question: Q: Which wireless security protocol offers the STRONGEST protection for a modern enterprise network?
questions.explanation: WPA3-Enterprise provides the strongest protection with improved encryption (SAE) and 192-bit security options. WEP and WPA are deprecated and insecure.
questions.hint: The newest standard with enterprise authentication.

questions.question: Q: A security policy requires that no single person can complete a critical transaction alone. Which principle does this enforce?
questions.explanation: Separation of duties divides critical tasks among multiple people to prevent fraud and error, ensuring no single individual has end-to-end control.
questions.hint: It requires more than one person to complete a task.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following hashing algorithms is considered cryptographically broken and should NOT be used for integrity?
questions.explanation: MD5 is vulnerable to collision attacks and is no longer suitable for security purposes. SHA-256, SHA-512, and SHA-3 remain acceptable.
questions.hint: This older 128-bit algorithm suffers from collisions.

questions.question: Q: An organization uses a cloud provider and is responsible only for its data and access management, while the provider manages everything else. Which cloud model is this MOST likely?
questions.explanation: In Software as a Service (SaaS), the provider manages the application and infrastructure; the customer manages only data and user access. The shared responsibility model shifts more to the provider as you move from IaaS to SaaS.
questions.hint: The customer manages the least in this model.

questions.question: Q: Which type of testing sends malformed or random data to an application to discover vulnerabilities?
questions.explanation: Fuzzing injects malformed, unexpected, or random inputs into an application to find crashes and security flaws such as buffer overflows.
questions.hint: It throws random garbage input at the program.

questions.question: Q: A company wants to verify a downloaded file matches the vendor's original. Which technique should they use?
questions.explanation: Comparing the file's computed hash to the vendor-published hash verifies integrity — any change to the file produces a different hash value.
questions.hint: A checksum comparison confirms the file is unchanged.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST description of a false positive in security monitoring?
options: A: A real attack that goes undetected
B: An alert triggered by benign activity mistaken as malicious
questions.answer: B: An alert triggered by benign activity mistaken as malicious
questions.explanation: A false positive occurs when a system flags normal activity as a threat. A false negative is a real threat that goes undetected.
questions.hint: The alarm fired, but there was no real threat.

questions.question: Q: Which authentication protocol uses tickets and a Key Distribution Center (KDC) for single sign-on in Windows domains?
questions.explanation: Kerberos uses a trusted KDC to issue time-stamped tickets, enabling secure single sign-on. It is the default authentication protocol in Active Directory.
questions.hint: It relies on tickets and a trusted third party.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a PRIMARY benefit of implementing a SIEM?
options: A: It encrypts all network traffic
B: It centralizes log collection, correlation, and alerting
C: It replaces the need for firewalls
D: It provides physical access control
questions.answer: B: It centralizes log collection, correlation, and alerting
questions.explanation: A Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system aggregates logs from many sources, correlates events, and generates alerts to support detection and investigation.
questions.hint: It aggregates and correlates logs from many sources.

questions.question: Q: An attacker floods a target with traffic from many compromised devices to make a service unavailable. What is this attack?
questions.explanation: A Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack uses many compromised systems (a botnet) to overwhelm a target, degrading or denying availability.
questions.hint: It comes from many sources at once to overwhelm a service.

questions.question: Q: Which document outlines the steps an organization takes to resume critical business functions after a disruption?
questions.explanation: A Business Continuity Plan (BCP) describes how an organization continues critical operations during and after a disruption. A Disaster Recovery Plan focuses specifically on restoring IT systems.
questions.hint: It keeps the business running during a disruption.

questions.question: Q: A developer stores an API key directly in publicly accessible source code. Which vulnerability does this represent?
questions.explanation: Embedding secrets like API keys in code exposes them to anyone with access to the repository. Secrets should be stored in secure vaults or environment variables.
questions.hint: The secret is baked directly into the code.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes tokenization?
options: A: Replacing sensitive data with a non-sensitive substitute value
B: Encrypting data with a public key
C: Compressing data to save space
D: Hashing data with a salt
questions.answer: A: Replacing sensitive data with a non-sensitive substitute value
questions.explanation: Tokenization substitutes sensitive data (like a card number) with a non-sensitive token, with the mapping stored securely elsewhere. Unlike encryption, tokens have no mathematical relationship to the original.
questions.hint: The real value is swapped for a meaningless placeholder.

questions.question: Q: An organization requires users to change passwords and reviews their accounts when they change roles. This process is part of which of the following?
options: A: Identity and access management lifecycle
questions.answer: A: Identity and access management lifecycle
questions.explanation: The IAM lifecycle covers provisioning, ongoing management, role changes, and de-provisioning of identities to ensure access remains appropriate over time.
questions.hint: It governs accounts from creation to deletion.

questions.question: Q: Which type of site provides a fully equipped, operational duplicate environment ready for immediate failover?
questions.explanation: A hot site is fully operational with hardware, data, and connectivity ready for near-immediate failover. Cold sites are empty space, and warm sites are partially equipped.
questions.hint: It is ready to take over almost instantly.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a key characteristic of the Zero Trust security model?
options: A: Trust all internal traffic by default
B: Never trust, always verify every request regardless of location
C: Rely solely on perimeter firewalls
D: Grant permanent access after first login
questions.answer: B: Never trust, always verify every request regardless of location
questions.explanation: Zero Trust assumes no implicit trust based on network location and continuously verifies identity, device posture, and authorization for each request.
questions.hint: Its motto is 'never trust, always verify.'

questions.question: Q: A user receives a text message with a malicious link claiming their bank account is locked. What type of attack is this?
questions.explanation: Smishing is phishing conducted via SMS text messages. Vishing uses voice calls, and whaling targets executives.
questions.hint: The 'SM' stands for SMS.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is used to securely store and manage cryptographic keys in tamper-resistant hardware?
questions.explanation: An HSM is a dedicated, tamper-resistant hardware device for generating, storing, and managing cryptographic keys at scale. A TPM serves a similar purpose but is typically bound to a single host.
questions.hint: A dedicated appliance for enterprise key management.

questions.question: Q: An IDS that only alerts on suspicious traffic but does not block it is operating in which mode?
questions.explanation: An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) monitors and alerts but does not block traffic. An Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) sits inline and can actively block.
questions.hint: It watches and warns but does not stop traffic.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes privilege escalation?
options: A: Sharing credentials with a coworker
B: Gaining higher-level access than originally granted
C: Logging out of an account
questions.answer: B: Gaining higher-level access than originally granted
questions.explanation: Privilege escalation is when an attacker or user obtains elevated rights beyond what was authorized, either vertically (higher role) or horizontally (another user's access).
questions.hint: Going from a regular user to admin without authorization.

questions.question: Q: A company implements geofencing so that certain data is only accessible within corporate premises. Which authentication attribute does this use?
questions.explanation: Geofencing and geolocation-based restrictions use the 'somewhere you are' attribute, granting or denying access based on physical location.
questions.hint: Access depends on physical location.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST mitigates the risk of cross-site scripting (XSS)?
options: B: Input validation and output encoding
C: Using a longer DHCP lease
questions.answer: B: Input validation and output encoding
questions.explanation: XSS is mitigated by validating and sanitizing input and encoding output so injected scripts are not executed by the browser.
questions.hint: Sanitize what users submit and what the page renders.

questions.question: Q: What is the PRIMARY goal of change management in security operations?
options: A: To prevent all changes to systems
B: To ensure changes are documented, reviewed, and approved to avoid unintended impact
C: To eliminate the need for backups
D: To bypass testing for speed
questions.answer: B: To ensure changes are documented, reviewed, and approved to avoid unintended impact
questions.explanation: Change management provides a structured process for reviewing, approving, testing, and documenting changes to minimize disruption and security risks.
questions.hint: Controlled, documented modifications reduce surprises.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is an example of data in transit?
options: A: A file saved on a hard drive
B: An email being sent across the internet
C: A document open in memory being edited
D: A backup tape in storage
questions.answer: B: An email being sent across the internet
questions.explanation: Data in transit is data actively moving across a network. Data at rest is stored data, and data in use is data being processed in memory.
questions.hint: The data is actively moving over the network.

questions.question: Q: A security analyst wants to prevent USB drives from being used to exfiltrate data on company endpoints. Which control is MOST appropriate?
options: B: Endpoint DLP with removable media controls
questions.answer: B: Endpoint DLP with removable media controls
questions.explanation: Endpoint DLP with device/removable-media policies can block or monitor USB usage to prevent unauthorized data transfer to external drives.
questions.hint: You need to control removable media on endpoints.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the concept of 'defense in depth'?
options: A: Relying on a single strong control
B: Layering multiple overlapping controls so no single failure is catastrophic
questions.answer: B: Layering multiple overlapping controls so no single failure is catastrophic
questions.explanation: Defense in depth uses multiple layers of overlapping controls (physical, technical, administrative) so that if one layer fails, others still provide protection.
questions.hint: Think multiple layers, like an onion.

questions.question: Q: An attacker intercepts and re-sends a valid authentication request to gain access. What type of attack is this?
questions.explanation: A replay attack captures valid data (such as an authentication token) and retransmits it to impersonate a user. Timestamps, nonces, and session tokens mitigate it.
questions.hint: Capture and re-send a legitimate transmission.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the correct order of volatility, from MOST to LEAST volatile, when collecting digital evidence?
options: A: Disk, RAM, CPU cache, backups
B: CPU cache/registers, RAM, disk, backups
C: Backups, disk, RAM, CPU cache
D: RAM, backups, disk, CPU cache
questions.answer: B: CPU cache/registers, RAM, disk, backups
questions.explanation: The order of volatility guides forensic collection from most volatile (CPU cache and registers, then RAM) to least volatile (disk, then archival backups) to preserve fragile evidence first.
questions.hint: Collect the data that disappears fastest first.

questions.question: Q: Which control provides accountability by recording which user performed which action?
questions.explanation: Audit logs record user actions and system events, providing accountability and supporting investigations. This is the 'accounting' element of AAA.
questions.hint: It creates a record of who did what.

questions.question: Q: A company classifies data as Public, Internal, Confidential, and Restricted. What is the PRIMARY benefit of data classification?
options: B: It ensures appropriate handling and protection based on sensitivity
C: It eliminates the need for encryption
D: It reduces the number of employees
questions.answer: B: It ensures appropriate handling and protection based on sensitivity
questions.explanation: Data classification assigns sensitivity levels so that protection, access, and handling requirements match the value and risk of the data.
questions.hint: It matches protection to the sensitivity of the data.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a benefit of using containerization over traditional virtual machines?
options: A: Each container runs a full separate OS
B: Containers are lightweight and share the host OS kernel
C: Containers require more resources than VMs
questions.answer: B: Containers are lightweight and share the host OS kernel
questions.explanation: Containers package an application and its dependencies but share the host kernel, making them lighter and faster to start than full VMs.
questions.hint: They share the host kernel instead of running a full OS.

questions.question: Q: An organization wants to prove the authenticity and integrity of a software update. Which mechanism should be used?
options: A: Code signing with a digital certificate
questions.answer: A: Code signing with a digital certificate
questions.explanation: Code signing uses the developer's private key to digitally sign software, letting recipients verify the publisher's identity and that the code has not been altered.
questions.hint: A digital signature on the executable proves its source.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes threat intelligence?
options: B: Evidence-based knowledge about threats used to inform defensive decisions
questions.answer: B: Evidence-based knowledge about threats used to inform defensive decisions
questions.explanation: Threat intelligence is curated, evidence-based information about adversaries, their tactics, and indicators of compromise, used to improve defenses proactively.
questions.hint: Curated knowledge about adversaries and their methods.

questions.question: Q: A security team blocks known malicious file hashes from executing on endpoints. This is an example of which technique?
questions.explanation: Deny listing (blocklisting) prevents known-bad items (hashes, IPs, apps) from running. Allow listing takes the opposite approach, permitting only approved items.
questions.hint: You are specifically blocking the known-bad items.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST reason to implement input validation on a web form?
options: A: To improve page load speed
B: To prevent injection attacks by rejecting malformed input
questions.answer: B: To prevent injection attacks by rejecting malformed input
questions.explanation: Input validation rejects malformed or malicious input, mitigating injection attacks such as SQL injection and XSS before the data reaches backend systems.
questions.hint: It defends against injection attacks.

questions.question: Q: An organization performs an assessment to identify and prioritize the potential impact of threats on business processes. What is this called?
questions.explanation: A BIA identifies critical business functions and the impact of their disruption, informing recovery priorities and metrics like RTO and RPO.
questions.hint: It determines what matters most to keep the business running.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a characteristic of asymmetric encryption?
options: A: It uses a single shared key
B: It uses a public and private key pair
C: It is always faster than symmetric encryption
D: It cannot be used for digital signatures
questions.answer: B: It uses a public and private key pair
questions.explanation: Asymmetric encryption (e.g., RSA, ECC) uses mathematically related public and private keys. Data encrypted with one key is decrypted with the other, enabling secure key exchange and signatures.
questions.hint: It relies on a mathematically linked key pair.

questions.question: Q: A company enforces a policy requiring employees to take at least one week of continuous leave each year to detect fraud. What is this control called?
questions.explanation: Mandatory vacation forces employees away from their duties so that irregularities or fraud may surface while others cover their responsibilities.
questions.hint: Forced time off can expose hidden misconduct.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the MOST effective defense against a brute-force password attack?
options: B: Account lockout policies and MFA
questions.answer: B: Account lockout policies and MFA
questions.explanation: Account lockout after failed attempts slows brute-forcing, and MFA ensures a guessed password alone is insufficient to gain access.
questions.hint: Lock accounts after failures and require a second factor.

questions.question: Q: What is the purpose of a Certificate Revocation List (CRL)?
options: A: To list all valid certificates
B: To publish certificates that are no longer trusted before their expiration
C: To generate new key pairs
questions.answer: B: To publish certificates that are no longer trusted before their expiration
questions.explanation: A CRL lists certificates revoked by the CA before their scheduled expiration. OCSP provides a real-time alternative for checking certificate status.
questions.hint: It identifies certificates that should no longer be trusted.

questions.question: Q: A security engineer configures a system to fail into a secure, locked state during a malfunction. This design principle is known as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Fail-closed (fail-secure) means that on failure the system denies access to protect security. Fail-open prioritizes availability by allowing access when a control fails.
questions.hint: On failure, it locks down rather than opening up.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following attacks exploits the trust a website has in a user's authenticated session by forcing unwanted actions?
questions.explanation: CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into submitting unwanted requests to a trusted site. Anti-CSRF tokens and same-site cookies mitigate it.
questions.hint: It abuses the user's already-authenticated session.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the role of an Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)?
options: A: It defines acceptable ways employees may use organizational resources
B: It specifies the network's IP addressing scheme
C: It lists all installed software versions
D: It replaces the incident response plan
questions.answer: A: It defines acceptable ways employees may use organizational resources
questions.explanation: An AUP sets rules for how employees may use company systems, networks, and data, and the consequences of misuse.
questions.hint: It governs how staff may use company resources.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a PRIMARY security concern with shadow IT?
options: B: Unapproved systems bypass security controls and create unmanaged risk
C: It reduces the attack surface
questions.answer: B: Unapproved systems bypass security controls and create unmanaged risk
questions.explanation: Shadow IT refers to unsanctioned hardware, software, or services that operate outside IT oversight, evading security controls and introducing unmanaged vulnerabilities.
questions.hint: Unsanctioned tech escapes IT oversight.

questions.question: Q: A company deploys sensors and agents to detect, investigate, and respond to threats on endpoints. Which solution is this?
questions.explanation: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) continuously monitors endpoints for suspicious behavior, enabling investigation and automated response to threats.
questions.hint: It focuses on detection and response at the endpoint.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the concept of risk transference?
options: A: Eliminating the risky activity entirely
B: Shifting the financial impact of risk to a third party, such as through insurance
C: Accepting the risk without action
D: Implementing controls to reduce the risk
questions.answer: B: Shifting the financial impact of risk to a third party, such as through insurance
questions.explanation: Risk transference moves the impact of a risk to another party, commonly via insurance or outsourcing. Other strategies include avoidance, mitigation, and acceptance.
questions.hint: Insurance is the classic example.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST way to protect data at rest on a stolen laptop?
questions.explanation: Full disk encryption (e.g., BitLocker) renders data unreadable without the decryption key, protecting information if the device is lost or stolen.
questions.hint: Encrypt the entire drive so a thief can't read it.

questions.question: Q: An attacker uses a list of common passwords against many accounts, trying one password across all accounts to avoid lockouts. What is this attack?
questions.explanation: Password spraying tries a few common passwords across many accounts, avoiding lockout thresholds that trigger from repeated attempts on one account.
questions.hint: One password tried across many accounts to avoid lockout.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following provides real-time validation of a certificate's revocation status?
questions.explanation: The Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) allows a client to query the current status of a certificate in real time, avoiding the need to download an entire CRL.
questions.hint: It queries certificate status on demand.

questions.question: Q: A security policy specifies how long different types of data must be kept before secure disposal. This is known as which of the following?
questions.explanation: A data retention policy defines how long data is stored to meet legal, regulatory, and business needs, and when it should be securely destroyed.
questions.hint: It defines how long to keep data before disposal.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is an indicator of compromise (IoC)?
options: B: Unusual outbound traffic to an unfamiliar external IP
D: A successful software license renewal
questions.answer: B: Unusual outbound traffic to an unfamiliar external IP
questions.explanation: Indicators of compromise are forensic artifacts (such as anomalous traffic, unexpected file changes, or unusual logins) that suggest a system may be breached.
questions.hint: It is evidence that a breach may have occurred.

questions.question: Q: Which mobile deployment model allows employees to use their personal devices for work?
questions.explanation: Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) lets employees use personal devices for work, raising challenges around data separation and control. COPE and CYOD involve company-provided devices.
questions.hint: Employees bring their own personal hardware.

questions.question: Q: What is the FIRST phase of the incident response process according to common frameworks?
questions.explanation: The incident response lifecycle begins with Preparation (policies, tools, training), followed by Detection/Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, and Lessons Learned.
questions.hint: Get ready before an incident ever occurs.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes 'security through obscurity'?
options: A: A strong, layered defense strategy
B: Relying on secrecy of design or implementation as the main security measure
D: Encrypting all data at rest
questions.answer: B: Relying on secrecy of design or implementation as the main security measure
questions.explanation: Security through obscurity depends on hiding details rather than robust controls. It is weak as a primary defense but can add a minor layer alongside real controls.
questions.hint: Hiding how something works, hoping no one figures it out.

questions.question: Q: An organization requires vendors to sign an agreement protecting sensitive information from disclosure. Which document is this?
questions.explanation: A Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) legally binds parties to protect confidential information from unauthorized disclosure.
questions.hint: It legally protects confidential information.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following technologies inspects and filters HTTP/HTTPS traffic to protect web applications from attacks like SQL injection and XSS?
questions.explanation: A WAF inspects application-layer (HTTP/HTTPS) traffic and blocks web-specific attacks such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting.
questions.hint: It specifically protects web applications at layer 7.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the term 'attack surface'?
options: A: The total number of employees
B: The sum of all points where an attacker could attempt entry or extraction
C: The physical size of the data center
D: The number of firewalls deployed
questions.answer: B: The sum of all points where an attacker could attempt entry or extraction
questions.explanation: The attack surface includes all exposed entry points — services, ports, interfaces, and accounts — that an attacker could target. Hardening reduces it.
questions.hint: All the ways in and out that an attacker could exploit.

questions.question: Q: A security analyst uses a tool to identify missing patches and misconfigurations without exploiting them. Which activity is this?
questions.explanation: Vulnerability scanning identifies known weaknesses, missing patches, and misconfigurations without actively exploiting them. Penetration testing goes further by exploiting findings.
questions.hint: It finds weaknesses but does not exploit them.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a benefit of using Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: A: It eliminates the need for any security
B: It provides consistent, repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure deployments
C: It requires manual configuration each time
D: It prevents all misconfigurations automatically
questions.answer: B: It provides consistent, repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure deployments
questions.explanation: IaC defines infrastructure in code, enabling consistent, automated, version-controlled deployments and reducing configuration drift and manual error.
questions.hint: Infrastructure defined in version-controlled files.

questions.question: Q: An organization wants to ensure only approved applications can run on its endpoints. Which control should it implement?
questions.explanation: Application allow listing (whitelisting) permits only explicitly approved applications to run, blocking unknown or unauthorized software by default.
questions.hint: Only pre-approved software is permitted to run.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the practice of collecting and analyzing publicly available information about a target?
questions.explanation: OSINT gathers intelligence from publicly available sources such as websites, social media, and public records, often used during reconnaissance.
questions.hint: Intelligence gathered from public sources.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following would BEST detect unauthorized changes to critical system files?
questions.explanation: File Integrity Monitoring baselines critical files and alerts on unauthorized modifications, helping detect tampering, malware, or misconfiguration.
questions.hint: It watches for changes to important files.

questions.question: Q: A user's credentials are stolen via a fake login page that mimics the real site. Which attack is this?
questions.explanation: A fake login page designed to capture credentials is a credential-harvesting phishing attack. User training and MFA reduce its effectiveness.
questions.hint: A counterfeit login page steals what you type.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the MOST appropriate control to reduce tailgating into a secure facility?
questions.explanation: An access control vestibule (mantrap) allows only one person through at a time, preventing an unauthorized person from following an authorized one (tailgating).
questions.hint: A two-door entry that admits one person at a time.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes a logic bomb?
options: A: Malware that self-replicates across networks
B: Malicious code that executes when specific conditions are met
C: A tool for encrypting disks
D: A type of firewall rule
questions.answer: B: Malicious code that executes when specific conditions are met
questions.explanation: A logic bomb is dormant malicious code that triggers when a condition is met (a date, event, or action), such as an employee's account being deleted.
questions.hint: It lies dormant until a trigger condition is met.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the PRIMARY purpose of network access control (NAC)?
options: A: To encrypt all files at rest
B: To enforce policy and assess device posture before granting network access
D: To back up user data
questions.answer: B: To enforce policy and assess device posture before granting network access
questions.explanation: NAC evaluates a device's compliance (patches, antivirus, configuration) and enforces access policies before permitting it onto the network.
questions.hint: It checks a device's health before letting it on the network.

questions.question: Q: An organization uses a formal, repeatable set of security controls published by NIST. Which of the following is an example?
questions.explanation: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework provides a structured set of functions (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) to manage cybersecurity risk.
questions.hint: A well-known risk-management framework from NIST.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST protects the confidentiality of data transmitted over a public network between two offices?
questions.explanation: A site-to-site VPN creates an encrypted tunnel between two networks over the public internet, protecting the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted data.
questions.hint: An encrypted tunnel between two locations.

questions.question: Q: A threat actor motivated by political or social causes defaces a company website to spread a message. What type of threat actor is this?
questions.explanation: A hacktivist is motivated by political or social causes and often uses attacks like website defacement or DDoS to promote a message.
questions.hint: Motivated by a cause, not money.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is TRUE about a stateful firewall compared to a stateless (packet-filtering) firewall?
options: A: It only inspects individual packets without context
B: It tracks the state of active connections and makes decisions based on connection context
D: It operates only at the physical layer
questions.answer: B: It tracks the state of active connections and makes decisions based on connection context
questions.explanation: A stateful firewall maintains a connection state table and evaluates packets in the context of established sessions, offering stronger filtering than stateless packet filters.
questions.hint: It remembers the context of active connections.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST method to securely dispose of a hard drive containing sensitive data before reuse in a low-security context?
options: B: Cryptographic erase or degaussing/physical destruction
C: Deleting files to the recycle bin
questions.answer: B: Cryptographic erase or degaussing/physical destruction
questions.explanation: Cryptographic erase, degaussing, or physical destruction render data unrecoverable. A quick format or deletion leaves data recoverable with forensic tools.
questions.hint: Deleting or formatting is not enough.

questions.question: Q: A company wants to allow partners to authenticate to its applications using the partners' own identity provider. Which technology enables this?
questions.explanation: Federation lets users authenticate through their home identity provider and access resources across trust boundaries, commonly using SAML or OIDC for SSO between organizations.
questions.hint: It links identity across organizational boundaries.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the security risk known as a race condition (TOCTOU)?
options: A: Running out of network bandwidth
B: A flaw exploited by acting between the time a resource is checked and the time it is used
questions.answer: B: A flaw exploited by acting between the time a resource is checked and the time it is used
questions.explanation: A time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition occurs when the state of a resource changes between validation and use, which an attacker can exploit.
questions.hint: The gap between checking and using a resource.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST control to ensure employees can recognize and report phishing attempts?
questions.explanation: Security awareness training educates users to identify social engineering and phishing, reducing the human-factor risk that technical controls cannot fully address.
questions.hint: The best defense against social engineering is educated users.

questions.question: Q: An organization implements a solution that brokers secure access and enforces policy between users and multiple cloud services. What is this called?
questions.explanation: A CASB sits between users and cloud services to enforce security policies such as visibility, compliance, data security, and threat protection across cloud usage.
questions.hint: It sits between users and cloud apps to enforce policy.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes elasticity in cloud computing from a security and availability standpoint?
options: A: The ability to automatically scale resources up or down based on demand
B: The permanent allocation of fixed resources
C: The encryption of all traffic
D: The physical destruction of drives
questions.answer: A: The ability to automatically scale resources up or down based on demand
questions.explanation: Elasticity allows cloud resources to scale dynamically with demand, supporting availability during spikes and cost efficiency during lulls.
questions.hint: Resources grow and shrink automatically with demand.

questions.question: Q: A security team simulates a real-world attack against the organization while a defensive team responds, testing detection and response. What is the attacking team called?
questions.explanation: The red team simulates adversaries to test defenses, while the blue team defends. The white team oversees the exercise, and purple teaming blends offense and defense collaboration.
questions.hint: This team plays the role of the attacker.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST reduces the risk of a single point of failure for a critical server?
options: A: Deploying it on a single power supply
B: Implementing redundancy and high availability (e.g., clustering, redundant power)
D: Reducing the number of network paths
questions.answer: B: Implementing redundancy and high availability (e.g., clustering, redundant power)
questions.explanation: Redundancy through clustering, load balancing, redundant power, and multiple network paths eliminates single points of failure and improves availability.
questions.hint: Add redundancy so one failure doesn't take everything down.

questions.question: Q: An attacker alters the ARP cache of hosts to redirect traffic through their machine. Which attack is this?
questions.explanation: ARP poisoning sends forged ARP messages to associate the attacker's MAC address with a legitimate IP, enabling interception of LAN traffic (an on-path attack).
questions.hint: It corrupts the mapping of IP to MAC addresses on a LAN.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the PRIMARY purpose of a playbook in security operations?
options: B: To provide a documented, repeatable set of steps for responding to a specific type of incident
questions.answer: B: To provide a documented, repeatable set of steps for responding to a specific type of incident
questions.explanation: A playbook defines standardized, repeatable response procedures for specific scenarios, improving consistency and speed, and it can be automated with SOAR.
questions.hint: A step-by-step guide for handling an incident type.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the concept of 'data sovereignty'?
options: A: Data is owned by the internet service provider
B: Data is subject to the laws of the country in which it is stored
C: Data can never be encrypted
D: Data must always be public
questions.answer: B: Data is subject to the laws of the country in which it is stored
questions.explanation: Data sovereignty means data is governed by the legal jurisdiction where it physically resides, which affects cloud storage location decisions and compliance.
questions.hint: The location of storage determines which laws apply.

questions.question: Q: A company automates repetitive security tasks and orchestrates responses across tools using a single platform. Which technology is this?
questions.explanation: Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) integrates tools and automates workflows and playbooks to speed and standardize incident response.
questions.hint: It automates and orchestrates response workflows.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of key stretching algorithms like PBKDF2 or bcrypt?
options: A: To make password hashing intentionally slower and more resistant to brute force
C: To transmit passwords in cleartext
D: To generate asymmetric key pairs
questions.answer: A: To make password hashing intentionally slower and more resistant to brute force
questions.explanation: Key stretching applies many iterations (and salting) to make password hashing computationally expensive, slowing brute-force and dictionary attacks.
questions.hint: It deliberately slows down hashing to hinder cracking.

questions.question: Q: An organization discovers an employee is stealing data before resigning. What category of threat actor is this?
questions.explanation: An insider threat originates from someone with authorized access — such as an employee or contractor — who misuses that access, whether maliciously or negligently.
questions.hint: The threat comes from someone already trusted inside.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following would provide the STRONGEST assurance of a user's identity during authentication?
options: B: A password plus a biometric plus a hardware token
questions.answer: B: A password plus a biometric plus a hardware token
questions.explanation: Combining something you know (password), something you are (biometric), and something you have (hardware token) provides strong multifactor authentication with three distinct factors.
questions.hint: Combine factors from three different categories.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST reason to use jump servers (bastion hosts) for administration?
options: B: To provide a single, hardened, monitored point of access for administering sensitive systems
questions.answer: B: To provide a single, hardened, monitored point of access for administering sensitive systems
questions.explanation: A jump server is a hardened, closely monitored host through which administrators must connect to reach sensitive segments, centralizing and controlling privileged access.
questions.hint: A controlled gateway for privileged administrative access.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the purpose of a Statement of Work (SOW)?
options: A: It defines the specific tasks, deliverables, and timeline for a project or engagement
B: It is a marketing brochure
C: It replaces an incident response plan
D: It sets employee vacation schedules
questions.answer: A: It defines the specific tasks, deliverables, and timeline for a project or engagement
questions.explanation: A Statement of Work details the scope, deliverables, milestones, and timelines of a specific engagement, often accompanying a master service agreement.
questions.hint: It spells out the scope and deliverables of an engagement.

questions.question: Q: A security analyst configures alerts to trigger only when multiple failed logins are followed by a success from a new location. This is an example of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Correlating multiple related events (failed logins plus a successful login from an unusual location) reduces noise and surfaces meaningful, higher-fidelity alerts.
questions.hint: Combining multiple events into one meaningful alert.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between authentication and authorization?
options: A: They are the same thing
B: Authentication verifies identity; authorization determines what the identity can access
C: Authorization verifies identity; authentication grants access
D: Both only apply to physical security
questions.answer: B: Authentication verifies identity; authorization determines what the identity can access
questions.explanation: Authentication confirms who you are; authorization determines what resources and actions you are permitted. Both are core to the AAA model.
questions.hint: One proves who you are; the other grants access rights.

questions.question: Q: An organization wants to detect if a mobile device has been rooted or jailbroken before allowing corporate access. Which solution provides this?
questions.explanation: MDM enforces policy on mobile devices, including detecting rooted/jailbroken status, requiring encryption, and enabling remote wipe before granting corporate access.
questions.hint: It centrally manages and enforces policy on mobile devices.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a PRIMARY characteristic of the 3-2-1 backup rule?
options: A: Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite
B: Keep 1 copy of data on the same server
D: Back up only once a year
questions.answer: A: Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite
questions.explanation: The 3-2-1 rule recommends three copies of data, on two different media types, with one copy stored offsite, improving resilience against loss and disaster.
questions.hint: Three copies, two media types, one offsite.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following attacks attempts to exhaust a system's resources by leaving many half-open TCP connections?
questions.explanation: A SYN flood sends many TCP SYN requests without completing the handshake, exhausting the target's connection table and denying service to legitimate users.
questions.hint: It abuses the TCP three-way handshake.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of a Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) program?
options: A: To align security activities with business objectives, manage risk, and ensure regulatory compliance
B: To configure firewall rules only
questions.answer: A: To align security activities with business objectives, manage risk, and ensure regulatory compliance
questions.explanation: GRC integrates governance (direction and oversight), risk management, and compliance to ensure security supports business goals and meets legal/regulatory obligations.
questions.hint: It ties together direction, risk, and regulation.

questions.question: Q: A company assigns a quantitative value representing the expected yearly cost of a specific risk. Which metric is this?
questions.explanation: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) equals Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) multiplied by the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO), estimating the yearly cost of a risk.
questions.hint: SLE multiplied by ARO gives you this yearly figure.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following controls would BEST prevent unauthorized devices from connecting to switch ports in an office?
options: A: Port security with MAC filtering / 802.1X
questions.answer: A: Port security with MAC filtering / 802.1X
questions.explanation: Port security limits which MAC addresses may use a switch port, and 802.1X requires authentication before a device gains network access, preventing rogue connections.
questions.hint: Authenticate or restrict devices at the switch port.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST description of steganography?
options: A: Encrypting data with a public key
B: Concealing data within another file, such as hiding a message inside an image
questions.answer: B: Concealing data within another file, such as hiding a message inside an image
questions.explanation: Steganography hides the existence of a message by embedding it within another medium (image, audio, video), unlike encryption which hides the content but not its presence.
questions.hint: It hides that a message even exists.

questions.question: Q: A vulnerability scan reports a flaw that does not actually exist on the system. What is this result called?
questions.explanation: A false positive is a reported vulnerability or alert that is not actually present or valid. Validating scan results reduces wasted remediation effort.
questions.hint: The scanner cried wolf when there was none.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the MOST important first step when responding to a confirmed malware outbreak on a workstation?
options: A: Reimage the machine immediately without analysis
B: Contain the incident by isolating the affected system from the network
C: Ignore it until end of day
D: Email all employees the malware sample
questions.answer: B: Contain the incident by isolating the affected system from the network
questions.explanation: Containment (e.g., isolating the host) limits the spread and damage of an incident before eradication and recovery, while preserving evidence for analysis.
questions.hint: Stop the spread before cleaning up.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the purpose of a digital certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) field?
options: A: To store the private key
B: To allow a single certificate to secure multiple hostnames or domains
questions.answer: B: To allow a single certificate to secure multiple hostnames or domains
questions.explanation: The Subject Alternative Name field lets one certificate cover multiple domain names or hostnames, which is common for services hosting several sites.
questions.hint: One certificate covering several hostnames.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is a key advantage of using multifactor authentication even if a password is compromised?
options: A: The attacker still needs the additional factor to gain access
C: The account is automatically deleted
questions.answer: A: The attacker still needs the additional factor to gain access
questions.explanation: With MFA, a stolen password alone is insufficient because the attacker must also provide the second factor, significantly reducing the risk of account takeover.
questions.hint: A stolen password alone is not enough.

questions.question: Q: An organization uses a framework that maps adversary tactics and techniques across the attack lifecycle to improve detection. Which framework is this?
questions.explanation: MITRE ATT&CK is a knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) that helps defenders map, detect, and respond to real-world attacker behavior.
questions.hint: A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following BEST describes the security benefit of network micro-segmentation?
options: A: It merges all systems into one broadcast domain
B: It isolates workloads at a granular level to limit lateral movement
D: It removes the need for authentication
questions.answer: B: It isolates workloads at a granular level to limit lateral movement
questions.explanation: Micro-segmentation applies fine-grained policies around individual workloads, strongly limiting an attacker's ability to move laterally after a breach — a key Zero Trust enabler.
questions.hint: Very fine-grained isolation to stop lateral movement.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following is the BEST practice when handling digital evidence to maintain its integrity in court?
options: A: Share it freely without tracking
B: Maintain a documented chain of custody
C: Modify timestamps to be consistent
D: Store it on the compromised host
questions.answer: B: Maintain a documented chain of custody
questions.explanation: A chain of custody documents everyone who handled evidence and when, preserving its integrity and admissibility. Hashing copies further proves the evidence is unaltered.
questions.hint: Track who handled the evidence and when.

questions.question: Q: A company wants to ensure that logs cannot be altered by an attacker who compromises a server. Which control BEST supports this?
options: A: Storing logs only on the local server
B: Forwarding logs to a secure, centralized, write-once log server
D: Disabling logging on critical systems
questions.answer: B: Forwarding logs to a secure, centralized, write-once log server
questions.explanation: Sending logs to a hardened, centralized, tamper-resistant (write-once/append-only) log server ensures that even if an endpoint is compromised, the evidence remains intact.
questions.hint: Get the logs off the box and make them tamper-resistant.

questions.question: Q: Which of the following describes the concept of 'implicit deny'?
options: A: All traffic is allowed unless explicitly blocked
B: All traffic is blocked unless explicitly permitted
questions.answer: B: All traffic is blocked unless explicitly permitted
questions.explanation: Implicit deny is a default-deny posture: anything not explicitly allowed is denied. It is a best practice for firewall and access control rule design.
questions.hint: Default to blocking; permit only what is listed.
